figures.caption: Figure 1. Biometric modalities used in Bharat ABIS: (a) 10-fingers (captured as 2 slaps and a thumb-pair), 2 irides and face. These modalities were selected to address the scale of the gallery (over 1.5 billion), ease of capture, and search accuracy requirements (FPIR and FNIR).

Figure 4. Face image variations due to ethnicity, social norms and religious practices. These are synthetic images that closely mimic the variability we observe in the Aadhaar dataset. The presence of head coverings, accessories and marks on the faces make the problem of face matching more challenging.
Figure 5. Examples of poor quality fingerprint images. These are synthetic images that closely resemble the challenges we observe in the Aadhaar dataset.
Figure 6. Examples of commonly seen eye conditions that lead to poor quality Iris capture.

Figure 8. FPIR vs FNIR plots of individual modalities and their combinations (both axes on log scale). At FPIR of 0.1%, unimodal FNIR values are 63.6% (Face), 60.3% (Fingerprint), and 85.5% (Iris). Combining all 10 fingers reduces FNIR to 1.3%; twomodality combinations yield 13.7% (Face+Irides), 0.51% (Fingers+Face), and 0.14% (Fingers+Irides). Fusion of all three modalities achieves FNIR of 0.01%. There is more than an order of magnitude reduction in error rate from single modality to two-modality combinations, with further reduction when fusing all three-modalities. Gallery size is 20M with ∼35K mated and ∼38K non-mated samples.

Figure 9. FNIR vs. FPIR plots for Bharat ABIS for various gallery sizes. At an FPIR of 1 in 1000, the FNIR (@gallery size) values are: 0.0015 (@1M), 0.002 (@5M), 0.0025 (@10M), 0.0028 (@20M), 0.004 (@50M), 0.012 (@100M), 0.022 (@150M), 0.03 (@200M), and .04 (@220M). As expected, for a fixed FPIR, FNIR increases with increase in gallery size. Both axes are on log scale.
